mission.title: Install and Configure Stacker on Kubernetes
mission.description: Stacker is a tool for building OCI images natively using a declarative YAML format. Installing Stacker allows you to create container images in a vendor-neutral manner, leveraging its rootless and hermetically sealed build capabilities.

steps.title: Add the Stacker Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Stacker Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add stacker https://stackerbuild.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Stacker using Helm
steps.description:
Install Stacker in a new namespace called 'stacker'. This command will deploy Stacker with the specified version.
```bash
helm install stacker stacker/stacker --version 1.1.6 --namespace stacker --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify Stacker installation
steps.description:
Check if the Stacker pods are running successfully in the 'stacker' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace stacker
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for Stacker
steps.description:
Update the Stacker deployment to set resource limits for better performance management.
```bash
kubectl set resources deployment/stacker --namespace stacker --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Port forward to access Stacker service
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Stacker service locally.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/stacker --namespace stacker 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Stacker will show the Stacker pods running in the 'stacker' namespace, and you will be able to access the service via port 8080 on your local machine. You can also verify that resource limits are applied to the Stacker deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add stacker https://stackerbuild.io/charts
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Stacker release from the cluster using Helm. This will remove all associated resources except for CRDs and persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall stacker --namespace stacker
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volumes that were created during the installation. This ensures that no leftover resources remain.
```bash
kubectl delete crd stackers.stackerbuild.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace stacker
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'stacker' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace stacker
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the Stacker deployment. This can be done by exporting the current Helm release values.
```bash
helm get values stacker --namespace stacker > stacker-backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then upgrade the Stacker release to the desired version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade stacker stacker/stacker --version 1.1.7 --namespace stacker
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check the status of the Stacker pods to ensure they are running with the new version.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace stacker
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Stacker pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the specific pod to diagnose the issue.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace stacker
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Stacker service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly and that the service is running. Check the service status.
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace stacker
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If the Stacker deployment fails due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set on the deployment. You may need to adjust them or allocate more resources to the cluster.
```bash
kubectl describe deployment stacker --namespace stacker
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to a version conflict, ensure that you are using a compatible version of the Stacker chart. Check the available versions in the Helm repo.
```bash
helm search repo stacker --namespace stacker
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and Helm installed to deploy Stacker.
